text: This is a robotic assisted laparoscopic interparietal hernia repair after kidney and liver transplantation.

This patient was a fifty nine year old woman with past history of end stage liver disease complicated by hepatorenal syndrome who had undergone a simultaneous liver and kidney transplantation in twenty seventeen and later presented in twenty nineteen with a year long history of intermittent right lower quadrant abdominal pain.
Physical exam revealed a palpable bulge in her right lower quadrant associated with her prior kidney transplant incision.
The CT scan image shows the transplanted kidney in the patient's abdomen, with no visible signs of a hernia in the right lower quadrant.

While the patient's imaging was not conclusive for evidence of hernia, given her symptoms and the presence of a bulge on physical exam, it was decided that the patient should proceed with a diagnostic laparoscopy and possible robotic assisted laparoscopic abdominal wall reconstruction.
The surgeon inserts a 5mm optical entry trocar into the left upper quadrant of the abdomen to gain access for the robotic-assisted laparoscopic procedure.
Two 8mm robotic trocars are placed in the left hemiabdomen, and the entry port is upsized to an 8mm trocar.
The surgical exploration reveals a 7x6 centimeter intraparietal hernia extending through the transversus abdominis and internal oblique muscles, while the external oblique muscle remains intact.
The surgeon performs moderate lysis of adhesions, revealing the abdominal wall defect in the right abdomen.

The surgeon creates a preperitoneal plane starting on the left side of the midline and extends it across the midline. On the right side, the posterior rectus sheath is incised to enter the retrorectus plane, which is then extended laterally. The lateral posterior rectus sheath is incised, allowing re-entry into the preperitoneal plane.
The surgeon dissects the preperitoneal plane near the abdominal wall defect, carefully separating the peritoneum to reveal the intact external oblique muscle.
The surgeon completes the release of the peritoneum from the area of weakness in the abdominal wall, fully separating it from the underlying structures.

The surgeon dissects over the transplanted kidney to create lateral overlap for mesh placement. After achieving the necessary overlap, the fascial defect is closed with a running number one Stratafix barbed suture.
The surgeon inserts an 18 by 5 centimeters soft barred mesh over the abdominal wall defect to reinforce the primary repair, securing it with interrupted 2-0 Vicryl sutures.
A 5mm trocar is inserted into the right upper quadrant, facilitating the introduction of a 19 French Blake drain into the extraperitoneal space next to the mesh.
The surgeon closes the peritoneum using a running two O V-lock barbed suture, completing the repair in the area of the abdominal wall defect.
The surgeon opens the surgical drain to the air, facilitating the evacuation of CO2 from the extraperitoneal space.
The patient had an uneventful post-operative course. She was advanced to a regular diet without incident and discharged on post-op day two with her drain still in place.
At her post-operative visit on post-op day ten, she was doing very well, denied significant pain, and had returned to moderate activity.
